title: Whole grilled sea bream
subtitle: Greek-style whole fish grilled with olive oil, lemon, and oregano
excerpt: Whole sea bream scored, rubbed with olive oil, stuffed with lemon slices and oregano, and grilled or roasted until the skin is crisp and charred and the flesh lifts cleanly from the bone. Finished with good olive oil, lemon juice, and dried oregano. The simplest serious fish dish.
sourceNotes: Whole grilled fish is one of the oldest cooking techniques in the Greek repertoire, documented from Homeric sources. Sea bream (tsipoura, τσιπούρα) is one of the most common fish in Greek aquaculture and coastal waters, and is the default whole grilled fish of Greek taverna cooking. The combination of olive oil, lemon, and dried oregano is universal.

content.text: A whole fish needs scoring (three diagonal cuts per side, down to the bone) so the heat reaches the thickest part of the flesh before the skin burns. The skin must be completely dry before it hits the grill; pat it thoroughly with kitchen paper and leave uncovered in the fridge for an hour if you can. Wet skin steams rather than crisping.

The fish is done when the flesh at the thickest score mark is white and opaque and pulls cleanly from the bone. At the right temperature this takes 7 to 9 minutes per side on a hot griddle or grill. The ladolemono (Greek oil-and-lemon dressing) goes on at the table, not in the pan. Hot oil added straight from the grill dilutes the fresh lemon flavour.

items.prepNote: ~400 g each, scaled, gutted, and scored 3 times per side
1 sliced thin for stuffing, 1 for juice in the dressing

content.text: Pat the {{sea-bream}} completely dry inside and out with kitchen paper. Score each fish three times per side at a diagonal, cutting down to the bone. Season the cavity and the scores with salt and pepper. Stuff each cavity with {{lemon}} slices and {{garlic}} slices. Brush the outside with half the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}}.

Heat a barbecue or griddle pan until very hot — it should smoke. Place the fish on the grill and cook for 7 to 9 minutes without moving. The fish should release cleanly from the bars when ready. Turn carefully using two spatulas and cook the other side for 7 to 8 minutes. Transfer to a serving plate.
Whisk the remaining {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} with the juice of the second {{lemon}}. Pour over the fish, scatter {{oregano-dried}} and {{parsley-flat}} over, and serve immediately.

items.symptom: Skin has stuck to the grill and torn
items.cause: Fish was not dry enough, or moved too soon
items.fix: Always pat completely dry; oil the fish not the grill; wait for the fish to release naturally — it takes 7 minutes at high heat

items.symptom: Flesh is raw at the bone despite cooked exterior
items.cause: Scores not deep enough, or fish too thick for the heat used
items.fix: Scores must reach the bone — 5 mm deep; larger fish take longer; finish in a 200°C oven for 5 minutes if needed

items.symptom: Fish has dried out and flesh is mealy
items.cause: Overcooked, or too much oil causing the heat to conduct unevenly
items.fix: Sea bream is done quickly — 7 to 9 minutes per side at high heat; use a moderate brush of oil, not a heavy coat
The grill must be at maximum; cook the first side without lifting the fish for the full 7 minutes

content.text: Whole grilled sea bream is the fish taverna standard of the Greek islands and mainland coast: the default order when the fish is fresh, presented whole, drizzled with ladolemono (lemon-oil dressing) and served with nothing more than a salad and bread. The simplicity is the point — it requires good fish and a hot grill, and beyond that there is nothing to improve on. Greek aquaculture produces large quantities of farmed sea bream (and sea bass, which can be substituted directly), making both fish available across Europe year-round, though the flavour of wild or line-caught fish from cleaner Mediterranean waters is noticeably better.
